title: Ratatouille
subtitle: Provençal vegetable stew, slow-cooked until meltingly soft.
excerpt: Aubergine, courgette, peppers and tomatoes cooked low and slow in olive oil until they collapse into a silky, fragrant stew. The technique of cooking each vegetable separately before combining is the key to avoiding a watery muddle. Serves four as a main or six as a side.
sourceNotes: Provençal ratatouille. The technique of cooking each vegetable separately before combining is associated with chef Jacques Pépin and Julia Child; the original village versions cooked everything together. Pre-1928 references to ratouilhou appear in Provençal culinary texts. The present recipe follows the modern separated-vegetable method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes excellently for up to three months. Texture becomes slightly softer after freezing, which is fine.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make a large batch in summer and freeze in portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made a day ahead; the flavour improves significantly overnight. Serve at room temperature or reheat gently.

content.text: The common mistake with ratatouille is cooking everything together from the start. Each vegetable has a different water content and a different cooking time; thrown in together they steam and stew rather than soften separately. The result is a grey, watery mush. The fix is twenty minutes of extra work: cook each vegetable in a hot, oiled pan before combining.

Salt the aubergine first if you have time; it draws out bitter moisture and firms the flesh slightly so it holds together in the stew. Ten minutes is enough. The final dish should be silky, fragrant with herbs, and collapsing at the touch of a spoon without being a purée.

items.prepNote: ripe, roughly chopped; or one 400 g tin
total; used across multiple cooking stages
for seasoning and drawing out aubergine moisture

content.text: Toss {{aubergine}} cubed aubergine with 1 teaspoon of the salt. Spread on a tray and leave for 15 minutes. Pat dry with kitchen paper.

Heat 2 tablespoons of {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large casserole over medium-high heat. Add the aubergine cubes and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until golden and tender, stirring occasionally. Transfer to a bowl. Repeat with the {{courgette}} courgettes in another 2 tablespoons of oil (6 to 8 minutes), then the {{pepper-red}} peppers (5 minutes). Set each batch aside separately.

In the same pan, heat the remaining oil over medium heat. Cook {{onion}} sliced onion for 8 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic, {{thyme}} of thyme and {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves; cook for 2 minutes. Add {{tomato}} of chopped tomatoes and cook for 5 minutes until broken down. Return all the cooked vegetables to the pot, stir gently, season with the remaining salt, and press the lid down. Cook over very low heat for 25 to 30 minutes until everything is very tender and fragrant.
Remove the bay leaves and thyme stems. Taste and adjust seasoning. Tear over basil if using. Leave to rest off the heat for 10 minutes before serving; the flavours settle as it sits.

items.cause: Vegetables cooked together rather than separately, or too much moisture in the tomatoes.
items.fix: Cook each vegetable separately first. Use fresh ripe tomatoes in summer; if using tinned, let the sauce reduce for longer uncovered.

items.symptom: Aubergine absorbed all the oil and tastes greasy
items.cause: Aubergine cooked in insufficiently hot oil, or not salted first.
items.fix: Salt the aubergine to remove moisture. Cook in a hot, well-oiled pan; aubergine will absorb oil if the heat is too low.

items.symptom: Vegetables have lost their colour and look grey
items.cause: Overcooked, or cooked at too high a heat after combining.
items.fix: Once combined, cook on very low heat with the lid on. The goal is collapsing and fragrance, not browning.

content.text: Ratatouille comes from Nice, where it was a peasant dish of the summer harvest: a way of dealing with an abundance of aubergines, courgettes and peppers when they all arrive at once. It is served as a main course over rice, as a side with lamb or roast chicken, or cold the next day on toast with a fried egg on top. In the South of France, a pot is made on Sunday and eaten through the week.
